title: De/Het, Gender & Articles
situation: Foundation: the Dutch article system, adjective agreement, and definite vs indefinite

phrases.hindi: de straat, de deur, de kamer, de school, de winkel, de tuin, de trein
phrases.english: the street, the door, the room, the school, the shop, the garden, the train
phrases.context: These are 'de-words' (common gender). Most Dutch nouns are de-words. There's no reliable rule, you must learn each noun's article. Always learn: word + article together.
phrases.pronunciation: de STRAAT, de DUR, de KAA-mer, de SKOOHL, de WIN-kel, de TOWN, de TRAYN

phrases.hindi: het jaar, het woord, het nummer, het vliegtuig, het dorp, het brood, het hondje
phrases.english: the year, the word, the number, the airplane, the village, the bread, the little dog
phrases.context: 'Het-words' are neuter gender, roughly 1/3 of Dutch nouns. 'Hondje' is het because ALL diminutives (-je) are het. This is the ONE reliable rule: diminutive = always het.
phrases.pronunciation: het YAAR, het WOORT, het NUM-mer, het FLEECH-towch, het DORP, het BROHT, het HON-tye

phrases.hindi: een man, een vrouw, een kind, een huis, geen man, geen vrouw
phrases.english: a man, a woman, a child, a house, no man, no woman
phrases.context: Indefinite article: 'een' (pronounced 'uhn') for ALL nouns, no de/het distinction! 'Geen' = not a / no. So the de/het distinction only matters with definite article 'de/het'.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn MAN, uhn VROW, uhn KINT, uhn HOWS, chayn MAN, chayn VROW

phrases.hindi: de grote man, het grote huis, een grote man, een groot huis
phrases.english: the big man (de), the big house (het), a big man, a big house
phrases.context: CRITICAL adjective rule: after 'de' and 'een + de-word' / add -e. After 'het' + indefinite ('een') / NO -e. 'Een groot huis' (no -e) but 'een grote man' (has -e)
phrases.pronunciation: de CHROH-te MAN, het CHROH-te HOWS, uhn CHROH-te MAN, uhn CHROHT HOWS

phrases.hindi: de mannen, de vrouwen, de kinderen, de huizen, de boeken, de auto's
phrases.english: the men, the women, the children, the houses, the books, the cars
phrases.context: ALL plurals use 'de'. 'De kinderen' even though 'het kind'. Plural endings: -en (most words), -s (after -el, -em, -en, -er, short vowel + consonant), -eren (child/egg/etc)
phrases.pronunciation: de MAN-nen, de VROW-wen, de KIN-der-en, de HOW-zen, de BOO-ken, de OW-tohs

phrases.hindi: Deze trein is snel. Dit vliegtuig is groot. Dit zijn de kinderen. Is dit jouw nummer?
phrases.english: This train is fast. This airplane is big. These are the children. Is this your number?
phrases.context: Demonstratives agree with gender: 'deze/die' (this/that) with de-words singular, 'dit/dat' with het-words singular, and 'dit/dat zijn' for plural. Note: in the copular pattern 'dit is X' / 'dat is X' ('this/that is X'), Dutch defaults to dit/dat regardless of X's gender. 'Jouw' = your (informal)
phrases.pronunciation: DAY-ze TRAYN is SNEL. dit FLEECH-towch is CHROHT. dit zayn de KIN-der-en. is dit yow NUM-mer?

phrases.hindi: mijn boek, jouw fiets, zijn auto, haar huis, ons land, jullie stad
phrases.english: my book, your bicycle, his car, her house, our country, your (pl) city
phrases.context: Possessive pronouns: mijn (my), jouw/je (your), zijn (his), haar (her), ons/onze (our), jullie (your plural), hun (their). 'Ons' with het-words, 'onze' with de-words: 'ons land' but 'onze stad'
phrases.pronunciation: mayn BOOK, yow FEETS, zayn OW-toh, haar HOWS, ons LANT, YUL-ee STAT

grammar_notes: There is no reliable gender rule in Dutch, you must memorize each noun with its article. Good habit: when learning a new word, always learn 'de/het + word'
Reliable rules that DO exist: (1) All diminutives (-je, -tje, -pje, -etje) are always het. (2) All plurals are always de. (3) Infinitives used as nouns are het: 'het eten' (the eating/food)
Adjective -e rule: add -e EXCEPT for het-words in indefinite singular context. Memory trick: if you'd say 'een groot X', no -e. If you'd say 'de grote X' or 'het grote X', add -e
Gender matters for pronouns: de-words / hij (he) even for objects. het-words / het (it). 'De stoel' / 'hij staat daar' (the chair, he stands there)
culture_notes: Native Dutch speakers sometimes make de/het mistakes too, especially with less common words
The Van Dale dictionary (Dutch standard dictionary) marks each noun as de or het
In informal speech, people sometimes say 'de' for het-words, Belgians do this more often

skill_breakdown.explanation: Roughly 2/3 of Dutch nouns are de-words, 1/3 are het-words. No rule covers all cases. The one hard rule: diminutives (-je form) are ALWAYS het. Start by learning the most common nouns with their articles.

more_examples.english: het, diminutives of girl and boy
de school, but het schooltje (diminutive flips to het)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Add -e to adjectives except in ONE case: indefinite (een) + het-word + adjective. 'Een groot huis' (no -e). Everything else gets -e: 'de grote man', 'het grote huis', 'een grote man', 'grote mannen'.

practice_prompt: You are a Dutch tutor drilling the de/het article system, including ripple effects on adjective endings and demonstratives. Address u as `u`. Open in Dutch: `Goedendag! Laten we de/het oefenen. Eerste vraag: 'boek', de of het?` Give ONE task at a time, wait for u's answer. Run 6-8 items, escalating: pick de or het for a noun (~75% de, ~25% het; include a diminutive, ALWAYS het, and a plural, always de), then a sentence-completion on the adjective trap (`een mooi boek` vs `een mooie tafel`, no -e when een + het-word), then a demonstrative pick (deze/die for de-words, dit/dat for het-words), then a singular / plural conversion (article flips to de). Flag mistakes with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Watch for: wrong article (remind u that guessing `de` is right 75% of the time), `een mooie boek` (should be `een mooi boek`), `die boek` (should be `dat boek`), and defaulting to `de` for plurals (praise this, correct). Stay in Dutch unless u asks for English. End with `Heel goed! De/het zit vast. Tot ziens.`

theory.intro: Dutch has two genders hiding inside its articles: `de` and `het`. About three-quarters of all nouns take `de`, so when you guess, guess `de` and you'll be right 75% of the time. But `het` shows up often enough that you can't ignore it, and the article you pick ripples outward to adjective endings, demonstratives, and more. Unlike Hindi gender (where the noun ending gives a hint), Dutch gender has only weak surface patterns. Mostly you learn the article with every word. Painful upfront, mechanical afterwards.

sections.heading: Two articles, no foolproof rule
sections.body:
Dutch nouns split into two camps based on which `the` they take. About **75% are `de`-words** (common gender). Historically Dutch had separate masculine and feminine nouns, but those merged centuries ago into one big `de` bucket. The remaining **25% are `het`-words** (neuter). There's no signal you can read off the noun itself: `de tafel` (table) and `het boek` (book) look equally ordinary.

So there's only one workable habit: learn each new noun **with its article**. The word `huis` alone is useless. What you memorize is `het huis`. Write your flashcards as `de man` / `het kind`, not `man` / `kind`. Native speakers absorbed this as toddlers. As an adult, you'll get it through sheer repetition.

examples.breakdown: common gender, historically masculine, now just `de`
common gender, historically feminine, now just `de`
common gender, no biological gender involved, just `de`

callout.body: There is no shortcut. You can't predict de/het from the meaning of the noun (`de man` and `het kind` are both human; `de tafel` and `het boek` are both inanimate). Memorize the article with every new word, full stop.

sections.cutting_intro: Welcome! Dutch has two flavors of `the`: `de` and `het`. Which one a noun takes is mostly memorization. Let's get the lay of the land.

quick_check.question: Roughly what percentage of Dutch nouns are `de`-words?
quick_check.explanation: About 75% are `de`-words, 25% are `het`-words. So if you have to guess blind, guess `de`, you'll be right three times out of four.

sections.heading: Patterns that help (not rules)
sections.body:
There's no airtight rule, but certain endings tilt strongly one way. These patterns are about 80% reliable: useful as a first guess, but expect exceptions. The most useful by far is the diminutive (the little/cute `-je` form). Any noun ending in `-je`, `-tje`, `-pje`, or `-etje` is **always `het`**. No exceptions. This is as close as Dutch gets to a hard rule. A few other endings lean predictably:
- `-isme`, `-ment`, `-ium`, `-um` / usually `het` (often abstract or borrowed Latin/Greek terms)
- `-ing`, `-heid` / usually `de` (abstract nouns built from verbs or adjectives)
- language names / `het` (`het Nederlands`, `het Duits`, `het Engels`)
- country names / usually no article at all in normal use (`Nederland`, `Duitsland`)

table.caption: Endings and their gender tendencies
rows: het meisje, het kindje, het tafeltje
het Nederlands, het Engels, het Duits

callout.body: The diminutive rule is the most valuable single fact in Dutch gender. Any `-je` noun is `het`, period, even `het meisje` (the girl), where biological gender would suggest otherwise. Lean on it hard.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the patterns. Not rules, just leans. The diminutive one is gold. The rest are good first guesses.

quick_check.question: Which article does `meisje` (girl) take?
quick_check.explanation: `Het meisje`, because `-je` is a diminutive ending, and ALL diminutives are `het`. Biological gender doesn't override the grammatical rule.

sections.heading: Definite vs indefinite, and the plural reset
sections.body:
The de/het split only matters for the **definite** article (`the`). The indefinite article (`a` / `an`) is `een` for every noun, whatever its gender: `een tafel`, `een boek`, `een huis`. Same word, no choice to make.

Even better: all plurals take `de`, regardless of the singular's gender. `Het boek` is neuter, but `de boeken` (the books) uses `de`, just like `de tafels`. `Het kind` becomes `de kinderen`. Gender essentially disappears in the plural.

That's why gender matters most for singular definite nouns, and for the adjective endings and demonstratives those nouns trigger, which come next.

table.caption: Article forms across definiteness and number

callout.body: Plurals always take `de`. That means the gender of a noun mostly matters in three places: the singular definite article, the adjective ending, and the demonstrative (deze/die vs dit/dat). Everywhere else, it quietly disappears.

sections.cutting_intro: Quick relief. `Een` (a/an) works for everything, and all plurals take `de`. So gender only really matters for one singular `the`.

quick_check.question: What's the plural definite article for `het boek` (the book)?
quick_check.explanation: `De boeken`, even though `het boek` is neuter in the singular, all plurals take `de`. Gender disappears in the plural.

sections.heading: The ripple: adjective endings
sections.body:
Here's where gender starts paying out. When an adjective sits before a noun, it usually adds an `-e`. `Mooi` (beautiful) becomes `mooie` in `de mooie tafel`, `een mooie tafel`, `het mooie boek`, and `mooie boeken`. Easy enough: `-e` almost everywhere.

There's exactly **one case** where the adjective stays bare (no `-e`): before a `het`-word with the indefinite article `een`, or with no article at all.

- `een mooi boek` (no `-e`)
- `mooi water` (no article, `het`-word, no `-e`)

The same `het`-word with the definite article gets the `-e` back: `het mooie boek`. This is the classic adjective-ending error English speakers make. Drilling it early saves years of pain.

callout.body: The contrast `een mooie tafel` vs `een mooi boek` is the single most common adjective-ending mistake English speakers make. The pattern: a `het`-word + `een` (or no article) keeps the adjective bare. Everywhere else, add `-e`.

sections.cutting_intro: Heads up, this is the famous trap. Adjective endings depend on the article. Get it wrong and natives smile politely. Get it right and you sound careful.

quick_check.question: Which is correct: `a beautiful book` in Dutch?
quick_check.explanation: `Een mooi boek`, `boek` is a `het`-word, and with the indefinite `een`, the adjective stays bare (no `-e`). With `het mooie boek` (definite), the `-e` would come back.

sections.heading: Demonstratives: deze/die vs dit/dat
sections.body:
Once you know whether a noun is `de` or `het`, the demonstratives (this / that words) follow automatically. - `de`-words take `deze` (this) and `die` (that)
- `het`-words take `dit` (this) and `dat` (that) Memory trick: `de`-words get the d-e shapes (deze, die); `het`-words get the d-i / d-a shapes (dit, dat). This extends to standalone demonstrative pronouns too. To say `this is good`, you pick based on the noun you have in mind: a `de`-word in mind / `deze is goed`; a `het`-word in mind / `dit is goed`. The system is fully consistent. Learn the article once and the demonstrative comes free.

callout.body: If you know the article, you know the demonstrative, for free. `De` / `deze` / `die`. `Het` / `dit` / `dat`. This is one of the rewards for the upfront investment of memorizing each noun's article.

sections.cutting_intro: Final piece. `This` and `that` also track gender. But once you know the article, the demonstratives come free. Nice payoff.

quick_check.question: How do you say `this book` in Dutch?
quick_check.explanation: `Dit boek`, `boek` is a `het`-word, so it gets `dit` (this) or `dat` (that). `De`-words would get `deze` / `die` instead.

theory.wrap_up: De/het is the single biggest memorization load in Dutch. There's no clever rule, just a lot of words to learn one at a time. The good news: once you know a noun's article, everything else (adjective endings, demonstratives, sometimes pronouns) follows automatically. Memorize the article with every new noun, lean hard on the diminutive rule (`-je` = always `het`), and accept that the rest is rote learning that pays compound interest. The phrases below mix `de`-words and `het`-words on purpose, so you can drill spotting the difference in context.
